news_summary: Progress is being made in protecting children online through strengthened measures to combat child sexual abuse while balancing privacy regulations.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès sont réalisés en matière de protection des enfants en ligne grâce à des mesures renforcées de lutte contre les abus sexuels sur mineurs, tout en respectant l'équilibre avec la réglementation relative à la vie privée.
news_summary_es: Se están logrando avances en la protección de los menores en Internet gracias al refuerzo de las medidas para combatir los abusos sexuales a menores, al tiempo que se mantiene un equilibrio con la normativa en materia de privacidad.

articles.title: Press release - Combating child sexual abuse online: support for more limited ePrivacy derogation
articles.summary: On Thursday, Parliament adopted amendments to a privacy exemption allowing electronic communication services to voluntarily detect child sexual abuse.Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
In today’s vote on a derogation from ePrivacy rules for the purpose of detecting child sexual abuse online, MEPs adopted amendments to the Council position.

They want to exclude “communications to which end-to-end encryption is, has been or will be applied” from the scope of the law.

At this stage in the process (EP second reading), an absolute majority of MEPs - currently 360 – was needed to reject or amend the Council position. In an initial vote, a simple majority supported rejecting the position (314 in favour, 276 against, 17 abstentions). As there was no majority in favour of rejecting the amended EP position (276 in favour, 286 against, 30 abstentions), the second reading is closed.

The EP position (text as amended) will now be sent to the Council, which has three months to approve or reject the amendments. If the Council does not accept all of the amendments, EP and Council will move to conciliation to agree on the law.

The Council position would effectively have brought back a lapsed derogation allowing providers to voluntarily detect child sexual abuse (CSA) and solicitation of children in private communications on their services and remove and report relevant material.



Background and next steps



After the Commission proposed another extension to the derogation, MEPs voted to limit the scope of the detection measures and to require judicial approval. They then started negotiations with the Council, but no agreement was reached. On 26 March Parliament rejected the Commission proposal to extend the derogation and closed its first reading, leading to the expiry of the interim law on 3 April 2026. Subsequently, the Council decided to send the proposal back to Parliament for a second reading.

The derogation is meant to be a temporary measure to prevent a legal vacuum while talks on a permanent regime to combat child sexual abuse online are ongoing. Most aspects of the permanent law were agreed under the Cyprus Presidency of the Council in the first half of 2026, leaving certain aspects still to be discussed.

In parallel, MEPs and the Council have also agreed on updating a directive to combat child sexual abuse.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on legislative progress in the European Parliament to combat child sexual abuse online by adopting amendments that allow voluntary detection measures while protecting privacy through exclusions and judicial oversight. This represents a significant policy step with broad societal impact to protect children and uphold human rights in digital communications.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament advances privacy-safe measures to detect child abuse online
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Parliament is working on laws to fight child sexual abuse online while protecting people’s privacy. There was a temporary rule allowing electronic service providers to detect abuse, but it expired in April 2026. Now, lawmakers are trying to create new, safer rules.

**What happened** – On Thursday, Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) voted on changes to a privacy rule called ePrivacy. They agreed to allow companies to voluntarily detect child sexual abuse in their services, but with limits. For example, messages that use end-to-end encryption are not included. Also, any detection would need approval from a judge. The Parliament sent these changes to the Council, which has three months to accept or reject them.

**Impact** – This decision is important because it balances two things: protecting children from harm online and respecting the privacy of users. By excluding encrypted communications and requiring judicial approval, the rules aim to avoid unnecessary spying while still helping to find and stop abuse. This careful approach is a step forward in making online spaces safer for children across Europe.

**What's next step** – The Council will now review the Parliament's changes. If they don’t agree, both groups will meet to find a compromise. Meanwhile, talks continue on a permanent law to fight child sexual abuse online. There is also work to update another law that helps combat this problem.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has moved forward with privacy-conscious rules allowing voluntary detection of child sexual abuse online, aiming to protect children while respecting user privacy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen avance des mesures respectueuses de la vie privée pour détecter les abus sexuels sur enfants en ligne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Parlement européen travaille sur des lois pour lutter contre les abus sexuels sur enfants en ligne tout en protégeant la vie privée des utilisateurs. Une règle temporaire permettant aux fournisseurs de services électroniques de détecter ces abus a expiré en avril 2026. Les députés cherchent à créer de nouvelles règles plus sûres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, les députés européens ont voté des modifications à une règle de confidentialité appelée ePrivacy. Ils ont accepté que les entreprises puissent détecter volontairement les abus sexuels sur enfants dans leurs services, mais avec des limites. Par exemple, les communications chiffrées de bout en bout sont exclues. De plus, toute détection doit être approuvée par un juge. Le Parlement a envoyé ces changements au Conseil, qui a trois mois pour les accepter ou les rejeter.

**Impact** – Cette décision est importante car elle équilibre deux choses : protéger les enfants en ligne et respecter la vie privée des utilisateurs. En excluant les communications chiffrées et en demandant une approbation judiciaire, les règles évitent une surveillance excessive tout en aidant à repérer et arrêter les abus. Cette approche prudente est un progrès pour rendre les espaces en ligne plus sûrs pour les enfants en Europe.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Conseil va maintenant examiner les modifications du Parlement. S’ils ne sont pas d’accord, les deux institutions se réuniront pour trouver un compromis. En même temps, les discussions continuent pour une loi permanente contre les abus sexuels sur enfants en ligne. Un autre texte est aussi en cours de mise à jour pour renforcer la lutte.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a adopté des règles respectueuses de la vie privée permettant la détection volontaire des abus sexuels sur enfants en ligne, pour protéger les enfants tout en respectant la vie privée.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo avanza en medidas que respetan la privacidad para detectar abusos sexuales infantiles en línea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parlamento Europeo está trabajando en leyes para combatir los abusos sexuales infantiles en línea mientras protege la privacidad de las personas. Una norma temporal que permitía a los proveedores detectar abusos expiró en abril de 2026. Ahora, los legisladores buscan crear reglas nuevas y más seguras.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, los miembros del Parlamento Europeo (MEPs) votaron cambios en una norma de privacidad llamada ePrivacy. Acordaron permitir que las empresas detecten voluntariamente abusos sexuales infantiles en sus servicios, pero con límites. Por ejemplo, no se incluyen las comunicaciones con cifrado de extremo a extremo. Además, cualquier detección debe contar con aprobación judicial. El Parlamento envió estos cambios al Consejo, que tiene tres meses para aceptarlos o rechazarlos.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión es importante porque equilibra dos cosas: proteger a los niños del daño en línea y respetar la privacidad de los usuarios. Al excluir las comunicaciones cifradas y exigir aprobación judicial, las reglas buscan evitar una vigilancia innecesaria y al mismo tiempo ayudar a encontrar y detener abusos. Este enfoque cuidadoso es un avance para hacer los espacios en línea más seguros para los niños en Europa.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora el Consejo revisará los cambios del Parlamento. Si no están de acuerdo, ambos grupos se reunirán para encontrar un compromiso. Mientras tanto, continúan las conversaciones para una ley permanente contra los abusos sexuales infantiles en línea. También se está actualizando otra ley para combatir este problema.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo ha avanzado con reglas que respetan la privacidad para permitir la detección voluntaria de abusos sexuales infantiles en línea, buscando proteger a los niños y respetar la privacidad de los usuarios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a delicate, glowing lock shaped like a shield gently enclosing a small, stylized child figure made of light, surrounded by softly glowing digital threads symbolizing encrypted communication, set against a calm, abstract background of interwoven, muted blue and beige tones representing law and protection.
